notes: Serum amyloid A-1 protein; reviewed Swiss-Prot (SAA1_HUMAN), 122 aa. Canonical SAA1 — only one reviewed human SAA1 entry exists. Note: P02763 (flagged as a candidate in earlier notes) is ORM1/alpha-1-acid glycoprotein 1, not SAA1; that candidate was erroneous. P0DJI9 is the closely related paralog SAA2; the old accessions P02735–P02737 predate the SAA1/SAA2 split and have been absorbed into P0DJI9 (SAA2). SAA1 is the direct downstream effector transcriptionally induced by the P. micra SirTM → CEBPβ axis (Chang et al. 2026, Figure 6).

organism: Homo sapiens
notes: Interleukin-17C; reviewed Swiss-Prot (IL17C_HUMAN), 197 aa. Distinct cytokine from IL17A/B/D/E/F; no unusual paralog merges. Downstream transcriptional target induced by the P. micra SirTM → CEBPβ → SAA1 axis in colonocytes (Chang et al. 2026, Figure 7).

organism: Homo sapiens
notes: Interleukin-17 receptor A; reviewed Swiss-Prot (IL17RA_HUMAN), 866 aa. Canonical signaling receptor for IL17C on epithelial and immune cells. IL17C signals through an IL17RA/IL17RE heterodimeric receptor complex, not through the classical IL17A receptor (IL17RA homodimer). Adding this node connects IL17C — currently isolated in the STRING subnetwork because CEBPβ→IL17C is a transcriptional regulation rather than a PPI — to its biologically correct downstream partner. IL17C-IL17RA signaling activates NF-κB and MAPK in target cells, extending the SirTM→H2BC4→CEBPβ→SAA1→IL17C mechanistic cascade to its receptor-level endpoint. IL17RE is a co-receptor for IL17C specifically and may appear as a STRING shell addition in the next fetcher run. Citation: Song et al. 2011, Nat Immunol, PMID 21993849. Note: the user-suggested PMID 21822258 was a different paper (IL-17 mRNA stabilization via Act1/TRAF2); the correct Song et al. 2011 Nat Immunol paper establishing IL17C-IL17RE/IL17RA signaling is PMID 21993849, co-published alongside Ramirez-Carrozzi et al. 2011 (PMID 21993848).

notes: Histone H2B type 1-C/E/F/G/I; reviewed Swiss-Prot (H2B1C_HUMAN), 126 aa. Composite entry: five genes (H2BC4, H2BC6, H2BC7, H2BC8, H2BC10 — formerly HIST1H2BC/E/F/G/I under pre-2022 HGNC nomenclature) encode an identical 126-aa protein, all collapsed into P62807. Used here as the representative canonical H2B for the SirTM substrate because: (a) P62807 is the most-cited Swiss-Prot H2B reference; (b) Chang et al. 2026 used a pan-H2B antibody (Proteintech 15857-1-AP) recognizing a shared epitope, so the exact paralog ADP-ribosylated by SirTM in vivo is ambiguous — P62807 is a valid representative node. If paralog specificity is resolved in future work, expand to individual H2BC* entries.

organism: Parvimonas micra
notes: WP_029950202.1 (locus tag T262_RS0106475 in P. micra strain A293) is the reference strain ortholog of Gene_1539 in P. micra strain Pm-42, which was experimentally characterized by Chang et al. 2026 as an ADP-ribosyltransferase (SirTM) that modifies histone H2B in human colonocytes and drives colorectal cancer progression via the CEBPβ/SAA1/IL-17C pathway. Gene identity confirmed by exact match of forward qPCR primer 1539-F within the CDS and 20/22 match of reverse primer 1539-R within the CDS (primers from Chang et al. Supplementary Table S1). The protein is 97.9-100% conserved at the amino acid level across assembled P. micra strains (BLAST vs nr). Note: NCBI auto-annotation labels this protein as a SIR2-family deacylase based on domain prediction, but Chang et al. demonstrated experimentally that it lacks deacetylase activity and functions solely as an ADP-ribosyltransferase (Chang et al. 2026, Figure 5C).

organism: Peptostreptococcus stomatis
notes: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate aldolase class II (gene fba); 307 aa; unreviewed TrEMBL. Surface-displayed moonlighting glycolytic enzyme that acts as the sole characterized virulence factor of P. stomatis. Binds human integrins ITGA6/ITGB4 on colonocyte surface, activating ERBB2-MEK-ERK-p90RSK signaling and driving CRC progression and RTK-inhibitor resistance. Strain: DSM 17678 (HMP draft genome, BioProject PRJNA34073). Full cascade: FBA → ITGA6/ITGB4 → ERBB2 → MEK → ERK → p90RSK (Huang et al. 2024, Cell Host Microbe, DOI 10.1016/j.chom.2024.07.001). No LPS enzyme or dedicated adhesin effectors are characterized for P. stomatis in the current literature; FBA is the sole validated virulence factor.

organism: Homo sapiens
notes: Toll-like receptor 4; reviewed Swiss-Prot, 839 aa. Canonical pattern-recognition receptor for gram-negative bacterial LPS. Added to complete the innate-immunity / NF-kB arm of the host subnetwork; justified by the presence of LPS-bearing gram-negative organisms (including Fusobacterium nucleatum) in the eoCRC cohorts. Yang et al. 2017 (PMID 27876571, Gastroenterology) directly demonstrated F. nucleatum activation of TLR4 -> MYD88 -> NF-kB -> miR-21 in CRC cells and mouse tumors. TLR4 is a direct PPI partner of MYD88 and bridges gram-negative microbial signals to the IKBKB/NFKB1/RELA nodes already in this seed list.

organism: Homo sapiens
notes: Myeloid differentiation primary response protein 88; reviewed Swiss-Prot, 296 aa. Canonical TLR4 cytoplasmic adaptor; direct PPI partner of TLR4 (DD-domain interaction) and of IRAK4, linking TLR4 activation to TRAF6 -> IKBKB -> NF-kB signaling. Added alongside TLR4 to make the innate-immunity arm of the host network structurally complete. Same Yang et al. 2017 source (PMID 27876571) explicitly names the TLR4 -> MYD88 -> NF-kB axis.

# IL17C (Homo sapiens): Resolved 2026-04-12. Canonical entry is Q9P0M4 (IL17C_HUMAN,
#   197 aa, reviewed). No paralog ambiguity.
# H2BC4 (Homo sapiens): Resolved 2026-04-12. Using P62807 (H2B1C_HUMAN, 126 aa,
#   reviewed) as the representative canonical H2B node. P62807 is a composite entry
#   covering five genes with identical sequence (H2BC4/6/7/8/10; formerly HIST1H2BC/
#   E/F/G/I). Paralog specificity of SirTM ADP-ribosylation is unresolved in Chang
#   et al. 2026 (pan-H2B antibody used); P62807 is a valid representative. Expand to

# Gemella morbillorum EXCLUDED from PPI network: no virulence factor has been
#   experimentally characterized in an intestinal or colonocyte context. The organism
#   is consistently co-enriched with P. stomatis and P. micra in CRC cohorts
#   (Senthakumaran 2024, Mol Oncol, PMID 38970464), and our co-occurrence network
#   analysis shows the G. morbillorum–P. stomatis partnership as the most reproducible
#   oral pathobiont co-occurrence across 5/5 studies. Genomic virulence factor
#   predictions exist (García López 2020, Front Microbiol, PMID 32296407 — including
#   PsaA-like adhesin, phosphorylcholine pathway, C5a peptidase) but none have been
#   functionally validated in any intestinal model. G. morbillorum's role in this
#   project is represented through the co-occurrence network analysis rather than the
#   mechanistic PPI network. Revisit if experimentally characterized effectors emerge
#   in future literature.
# Dialister pneumosintes EXCLUDED from PPI network: no virulence factor has been
#   experimentally characterized for this organism in any disease context — intestinal,
#   oral, or systemic — despite Doan et al. 2000 (PMID 10921975) explicitly calling
#   for such studies 26 years ago. No resolution has appeared since. A comprehensive
#   literature search (2026-04-12) found: (1) only clinical case reports noting that
#   "systemic virulence remains unclear"; (2) one genome annotation paper (Park et al.
#   2019, Korean J Microbiol) predicting housekeeping proteases and efflux pumps via
#   automated PGAP — none functionally validated; (3) no experimentally characterized
#   adhesin, secreted effector, or dedicated virulence protein. The microbiome
#   association with CRC is real (Demirci 2021, Eurasian J Med Oncol, systematic
#   review; Wirbel et al. 2024, Nat Med), but purely observational. The LPS-TLR4
#   mechanism sometimes cited for Dialister is inferred by family-level analogy to
#   Veillonella parvula (Matera et al. 2009, PMID 19828771); no D. pneumosintes LPS
#   has been isolated or characterized. LPS is also not a single protein and cannot be
#   cleanly represented as a PPI node. D. pneumosintes' role in this project is
#   represented through the co-occurrence network analysis rather than the mechanistic
#   PPI network. Revisit if experimentally characterized effectors emerge in future
#   literature. (Previous scaffold rows LPS_biosynthesis_enzyme and adhesin removed;
#   TLR4 and MYD88 added as host nodes on their own merits — see above.)
